# Training-Free Interactive World Models

> We make an autoregressive video diffusion model interactive at real-time speeds: prompts can be injected mid-generation by interpolating prompt embeddings, and a stack of inference-time optimizations (spectral scheduling, fused kernels, pipeline parallelism) pushes generation faster than playback.

- **Authors**: Ray Kasichainula, Subha Vadlamannati, Bryan Dong, Gino Chiaranaipanich
- **Published**: Jul. 05, 2026

---

## Introduction

Video is one of the slowest — but most useful — generative mediums. A modern video diffusion model spends seconds to minutes of GPU time producing a clip that plays back in moments, and once sampling has started, the generation is locked in: you cannot change your mind halfway through. That combination of latency and rigidity keeps video generation firmly in an *offline* workflow — write a prompt, wait, watch, repeat.

We wanted the opposite: a video model you can **talk to while it generates**. Say "a dog running through a meadow", watch the dog appear in real time, then say "it's winter now" and watch the meadow frost over — without stopping generation, without retraining anything, and without the seams of stitching two clips together.

This post describes the system we built for the **2026 Inference-Time Compute Hackathon**. It has two halves:

1. **Training- and data-free interactivity.** A prompt-injection scheme for autoregressive video diffusion that conditions new chunks on a *linear interpolation between prompt embeddings*, so the scene transforms smoothly mid-stream. No fine-tuning, no adapter, no data — it works on top of any prompt-conditioned autoregressive video model.
2. **Real-time inference.** A stack of inference-time optimizations — spectral resolution scheduling, fused custom kernels, and two-GPU pipeline parallelism — that together push generation *faster than playback*: 700 ms of compute per 750 ms chunk of video, and **0.69 s end-to-end from voice to pixels**.

> **Demo examples from the live system:** "dog running in meadow" → *winter background replaces meadow* · "luxury car near beach" → *neon city replaces beach* · "beach scene" → *a storm erupts on the beach* · "dog running through meadow" → *dog becomes wolf in a winter landscape*.

The result feels less like a video generator and more like a **steerable world model**: a simulator whose state you can perturb with language while it runs. We close with why we think this direction — interactivity as pure inference-time compute — gets more interesting as compute gets cheaper.

## Training-Free Prompt Injection

### Setup

Our base model is **Causal Forcing++**, an autoregressive video diffusion model that generates video in **chunks of 3 latent frames** (0.75 s of video each).Causal Forcing++ was released June 1, 2026. Any prompt-conditioned autoregressive video diffusion model works — nothing in the method is specific to this architecture. Each chunk is denoised in 5 steps by a DiT conditioned on (a) the prompt embedding via cross-attention and (b) previously generated frames via a KV cache over a sliding context window. Voice input is transcribed with **faster-distil-whisper-large-v3** [@gandhi2023distil], so the interaction loop is: speak → transcribe → embed → inject.

### A simple idea: interpolate the prompt embeddings

The naive way to change the scene mid-generation is to hard-swap the prompt embedding between chunk $t$ and chunk $t+1$. This fails visibly: the model's KV cache still encodes the old scene, the new cross-attention conditioning contradicts it, and the video jump-cuts or collapses.

Instead, we condition each chunk during the transition on a **linear interpolation between the old and new prompt embeddings**:

$
\tilde{e}_t \;=\; (1-\lambda_t)\, e_{\text{old}} \;+\; \lambda_t\, e_{\text{new}},
\qquad \lambda_t: 0 \to 1 \text{ over the transition window}
$

Each successive chunk moves $\lambda_t$ further toward the new prompt, so the scene *morphs* rather than cuts: the meadow gradually frosts over instead of teleporting to winter. The KV cache is never invalidated — past frames remain valid context, and the model resolves the changing conditioning the same way it resolves any other temporal change.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
 P1["initial prompt e_old"] --> C1["chunk 1 (λ=0)"]
 C1 --"KV"--> C2["chunk 2 (λ=0)"]
 P2["injected prompt e_new"] -.-> C3
 C2 --"KV"--> C3["chunk 3 (λ=0.5)"]
 C3 --"KV"--> C4["chunk 4 (λ=1)"]
 P1 -.-> C1 & C2 & C3
```

### What didn't work

We spent roughly nine hours iterating on prompt-blending methods before landing on the scheme above. For posterity, the graveyard:

- **Naive hard swapping** of the prompt embedding — jump cuts and scene collapse.
- **Backfilling the KV cache** with the new prompt (as in LongLive [@yang2025longlive]) — designed for a different regime; interacted badly with our short chunks.
- **Sliding context-window interactions** — the transition point sliding out of the attention window mid-morph caused instability.
- **Sweeping coefficients $\alpha$ and $\beta$** that separately weight self-attention and cross-attention contributions during the transition — tunable but brittle.
- **Growing the attention window after the cut** — helped stability, and survives in the final recipe.

> The final recipe is embarrassingly simple — interpolate the conditioning, leave the cache alone, widen the window briefly after a switch. Every clever variant we tried lost to it.

## SPEED: Spectral Progressive Diffusion

Interactivity is only worth having if the video keeps up with you. Our first problem: denoising a full grid of video tokens costs $O(n^2)$ in self-attention, and every denoising step pays it at full resolution.

**SPEED (Spectral Progressive Diffusion)** restructures denoising to be *spectrally autoregressive in the frequency domain*: early steps operate on a low-frequency representation of the chunk (structure, layout, motion), and later steps progressively introduce higher frequency bands (texture, detail). Because early steps see far fewer effective tokens, their attention cost drops sharply, and the expensive full-resolution work is reserved for the final refinement steps where it actually matters.

On the Causal Forcing base model, SPEED yields a **10% throughput increase** with no training and no visible quality loss.

## Kernel-Level Inference Optimizations

To know where the milliseconds were going, we profiled a full generation step. The op breakdown:

| Operation class | Share of step time | What we did |
|---|---|---|
| Attention | 64% | FlashAttention 3 [@shah2024flashattention3] |
| Memory movement / elementwise | 24% | Fused custom kernels |
| GEMM / linear | 12% | Left to cuBLAS |
| Normalization | ~1% | Fused custom RMSNorm |

Two observations drove the kernel work. First, attention dominates — switching to **FlashAttention 3** captured significant savings there for free. Second, nearly a quarter of the step is *not math at all* — it's memory movement and small elementwise ops, each launching its own kernel and each making a round trip to HBM.

We wrote **fused kernels** that overlap compute with memory traffic and collapse chains of small ops into single launches:

- **Fused RMSNorm: 7.71× speedup** over the unfused baseline.Norm is only ~1% of the step, so the end-to-end effect is small — but it was the cleanest demonstration that the fusion approach works, and the same pattern generalized to the gate.
- **Fused SiLU gate: 1.68× speedup**, attacking the elementwise share directly.

## Pipeline Parallelism

Even with fast kernels, one GPU doing *everything* was not real-time. Generating 750 ms of video took **976 ms** when a single GPU ran both the DiT denoising and the VAE decode — slower than playback, which means choppy video no matter how you buffer it.

The fix is classic systems work: the two stages have no reason to share a device.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
 subgraph GPU0["GPU 0 — DiT generation"]
 A["noise → 5 denoise steps → latent (~700 ms)"]
 end
 subgraph GPU1["GPU 1 — VAE decode"]
 B["latent → RGB frames (~276 ms)"]
 end
 A --"FIFO queue over NVLink"--> B
 B --> C["display"]
```

- **GPU 0** runs the DiT: raw noise in, five denoising steps, latent tensor out — **~700 ms per chunk**.
- **GPU 1** runs the VAE decode: latent tensor in, RGB pixel frames out — **~276 ms per chunk**.
- A **FIFO queue over NVLink** connects them: while GPU 0 generates chunk $N$, it ships chunk $N-1$ to GPU 1 for decoding.

The VAE decode is now fully hidden behind the next chunk's generation. End-to-end latency drops by **28%**, and the critical path per chunk becomes just the 700 ms DiT pass — comfortably under the 750 ms of video each chunk contains.

> **700 ms of compute per 750 ms of video.** Generation now runs faster than playback — the whole point.

export const base = import.meta.env.BASE_URL.replace(/\/$/, "");

## Results

Putting it together, the live system:

- **Runs faster than real time** — 700 ms of compute per chunk containing 750 ms of video.
- **Responds in 0.69 seconds end-to-end** from spoken words to generated pixels (ASR → embedding → injection → first affected chunk).
- **Transitions smoothly** — prompt injections morph the scene (meadow → winter, beach → storm, dog → wolf) with no jump cuts and no cache resets.
- **Requires zero training and zero data** — the method touches only inference-time conditioning, so it applies to *any* prompt-conditioned autoregressive video generation model.

 
 
 
 "dog running in meadow" → winter background replaces meadow
 
 
 
 "luxury car near beach" → neon city replaces beach
 
 
 
 "beach scene" → a storm erupts on the beach
 
 
 
 "dog running through meadow" → dog becomes wolf in winter background
 
 

### Relation to prior work

Training-free semantic control of diffusion models is a rich area; our contribution is doing it *mid-stream in an autoregressive video model, at real-time speeds*:

- **Prompt-to-Prompt** [@hertz2022prompt] edits images by manipulating cross-attention maps — we modify **no attention maps**, only the conditioning embedding.
- **Attend-and-Excite** [@chefer2023attend] optimizes latents at sampling time to strengthen semantics — we require **no latent optimization**.
- **SEGA** [@brack2023sega] steers generation along semantic directions found in noise-estimate space — we need **no search for steering vectors**.

The interpolation scheme is strictly simpler than all three, which is what makes it fast enough to run inside a real-time loop.

## The Infinite-Compute Regime

The deeper reason we're excited about this line of work: **diffusion models thrive in the high-compute, low-data regime**, and inference-time compute is the resource whose price is falling fastest. Every optimization in this post — spectral scheduling, kernel fusion, pipeline parallelism — converts raw compute into *interactivity* without touching the model's weights or requiring a single training example.

Once a video generator is (a) real-time and (b) steerable mid-stream, it stops being a media tool and starts being a **learned simulator**. You are no longer rendering clips; you are perturbing the state of a world model with language and watching it respond. This is full-duplex conversation, but for video.

Some applications we find obvious in hindsight:

- **Video game experiences** where the world is generated, not authored — and reacts to what players say.
- **Learning experiences** where an explanation can be visualized, then transformed on request ("now show it in winter", "now slow it down").
- **An actually immersive metaverse** — persistent generated environments that respond to their inhabitants in real time.

All of this scales with inference-time compute, none of it with data collection. In the infinite-compute limit, the interactive world model is just *there*, waiting to be steered.

---

*Built at the 2026 Inference-Time Compute Hackathon by Ray Kasichainula, Subha Vadlamannati, Bryan Dong, and Gino Chiaranaipanich.*
